report: The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head versioning to the left with initial unilateral motor activity involving arm asymmetry: in some episodes, the right arm extends while the left arm flexes; in others, the right arm flexes and the left arm extends. These movements are accompanied by tonic stiffening and progress in some instances to bilateral rhythmic jerking and tonic-clonic activity, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout all observed events. In one episode, unilateral features persist without progression to bilateral convulsive activity.

The patient is lying in bed, initially appearing to be asleep with their head turned to the side and their hand covering their face. There is no observable seizure activity, including blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related movements. The patient remains still throughout the video.

The patient is lying on a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a nasal cannula in place. Initially, the patient appears to be resting, then begins to move their head and right arm, showing signs of unilateral arm flexion. The movement progresses to involve both arms, with the patient lifting their head and adjusting their position. At other times, the patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by either right arm flexion with left arm extension or right arm extension with left arm flexion, resembling fencer posturing. These episodes progress to include tonic stiffening and clonic jerks, during which the patient remains unresponsive. No other seizure activity such as lip smacking or head version is observed. The patient remains still throughout periods without these episodic movements.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare. There is no visible movement or signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient exhibits right arm flexion and slight head version to the right, and remains in a resting position throughout the observed period.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and extension. The movements appear unilateral without spreading to other body parts.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head version to the right. The movements are subtle, with no significant spread to other limbs or tonic-clonic activity observed.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head version to the right. The movements progress to involve more pronounced arm flexion, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head tilt and subtle arm movements. The patient remains unresponsive with minimal observable motor activity.

The patient is lying in bed, initially focused on a laptop. As the video progresses, the patient lifts and adjusts the laptop's position, pointing at the screen and demonstrating continuous interaction with the device. There is no observable seizure activity, including blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks.

The patient initially appears to be resting, then exhibits a blank stare followed by slight head movement. There is no significant motor activity or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements observed.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the right. No significant motor signs such as arm flexion, extension, or clonic jerks are observed, and there is no spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the video.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with minimal or no movement throughout the video. A blank stare is observed initially, accompanied by subtle head movement and slight arm flexion in one instance; however, there are no other signs of seizures such as lip smacking, head version, or repetitive arm movements. The patient remains unresponsive and largely still, with no significant change in position or observable activity during the observation period.

The patient initially appears unresponsive with a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and subtle left arm flexion. The movements are minimal and localized without progression to more pronounced tonic or clonic activity.

The patient lies in bed with a blank stare, followed by slight head movement and subtle arm flexion. No significant arm extension or clonic jerks are observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout.

The patient initially appears to be reading a book, then exhibits a blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension. The head turns slightly to the left, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode. A subsequent event involves a blank stare with right arm flexion and left arm extension, during which the patient continues to show no responsiveness.

The patient is seated or lying on a bed with EEG electrodes attached to their head, and in some cases, an arm. The patient appears calm, cooperative, still, and unresponsive throughout the observation period, with no visible signs of seizure activity in several segments. Specifically, there are no observable manifestations such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related movements in these instances. In multiple observed episodes, the patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by motor phenomena that vary in laterality and progression. These include rhythmic clonic movements isolated to the right arm, left arm flexion with right arm extension resembling fencer posturing, and unilateral head version concurrent with ipsilateral arm flexion (either to the left or right). Some episodes progress from initial focal features—such as head version and unilateral arm flexion—to more pronounced asymmetric posturing or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity involving both arms. During all symptomatic episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. Two distinct types of observations are present: one showing no clinical seizure activity despite continuous monitoring, and another documenting evolving focal seizures with variable lateralized onset and progression, but consistently featuring impaired awareness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, with some segments also noting rightward head version or head turning to the right. In several instances, this progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. One segment describes early signs of seizure activity with subtle increase in muscle tone in the right arm and slight head turn to the right, without clonic jerks or bilateral involvement. Another observation notes head tilt backward with slight left arm extension and no tonic stiffening or clonic jerks. In contrast, one segment shows the patient restrained with only right arm flexion beginning as the video progresses, while the left arm remains secured and no further seizure activity is observed. Additionally, there is an episode where the patient is unresponsive with no observable movement, blank stare, or other seizure signs such as lip smacking, head version, or limb movements, remaining still throughout. A separate observation confirms the patient is lying on a bed with a nasal cannula, showing no signs of seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, followed by head version and asymmetric limb movements. In one instance, head version to the left is accompanied by left arm flexion and right arm extension, forming a fencer-like posture, without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. In another episode, rightward head version occurs with right arm extension. Additional observations include rhythmic head movements and bilateral arm involvement with clonic jerks progressing from initial arm flexion; the patient remains unresponsive during these motor events. At other times, the patient is observed lying still on the hospital bed with head turned to the side, showing no signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, or limb movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning (or version) to the left and left arm flexion. In one observation, the episode progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. In other instances, the movements remain localized without spread to other body parts, and there are no automatisms such as lip smacking. During one period, the patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive and holding a stuffed animal, with no observable seizure activity—including no blank stare, head version, arm movements, or other motor signs—and remains still throughout.

The patient, seated at a table, initially appears focused on coloring. Suddenly, they exhibit a blank stare, followed by a leftward head version and left arm flexion. The patient then points with their right index finger, suggesting a possible seizure episode.

The patient is seated in a relaxed position, showing no signs of a seizure such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or arm flexion/extension. The patient appears calm and is interacting with the healthcare staff.

The patient is seated in a relaxed position, appearing calm with no visible signs of seizure activity, including blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient is conversing with someone off-camera.

The patient is seated, initially in a hospital bed and later in a hospital chair, holding a can in their right hand and then a tube connected to their chest. The patient appears engaged in routine activities, including eating or drinking and a medical procedure, with no observable signs of seizure activity throughout. There is no evidence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related movements. The patient remains calm, focused, and stable during the observation.

The patient is seated, shirtless, with a bandage on the head, holding a device over the chest. No overt seizure activity is observed; the patient remains still and focused on the device throughout the video. No blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or limb flexion/extension is evident.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare and no observable movement. There is no visible seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, or rhythmic jerks. The patient remains still throughout the video.

The patient is lying in bed, holding a remote control. At times, the patient appears to be watching television or conversing/reacting to something, indicating periods of responsiveness. At other times, the patient appears unresponsive with a blank stare, yet remains still throughout all observed segments. There are no observable signs of seizure activity, including no blank stare (except where noted), lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. No seizure-related movements are visible during any segment.

The patient is lying in bed, holding a device in both hands, and appears to be focused on and engaged with it. There are no observable signs of a seizure, including blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or arm flexion/extension. The patient remains relatively still throughout the video.

The patient is lying in bed, variously holding a book or remote control, or with a book resting on the abdomen. The patient is shirtless in some observations, with a bandage on the head and a medical device attached to the chest. The right hand is frequently placed on the chest or exhibits slight movement, including brief flexion of the right arm in one episode. A blank stare is noted in multiple segments, sometimes followed by lip smacking, right arm flexion, and rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the right arm, without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or other automatisms such as head version. No observable tonic stiffening, clonic jerks (outside of the noted right arm activity), or generalized seizure activity is present in most observations. The patient remains unresponsive to surroundings during episodes of blank stare and minimal motor activity, while appearing calm and in a resting state at other times. Overall, intermittent signs suggestive of possible early or focal seizure activity are observed, but no sustained or generalized seizures are documented across the video.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare in multiple observations. During one episode, the blank stare was followed by lip smacking and head version to the right, without progression to tonic-clonic movements or other automatisms. In other instances, there were no visible signs of seizure activity, including absence of head version, lip smacking, arm flexion/extension, or clonic jerks. The patient remained still throughout these periods, with no observable motor activity. At another time, the patient was shirtless, with a bandage on the head and a hospital gown partially covering the body, and had electrodes attached to the chest. In a separate observation, food was scattered on the patient’s chest, and they were holding a remote control, making occasional hand movements and displaying facial expressions consistent with watching television, indicating periods of responsiveness.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with eyes closed and hands clasped over the chest, remaining still throughout the initial observation with no signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or arm movements. Subsequently, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic movements involving the hands and arms bilaterally, with repetitive motion and the right hand repeatedly approaching the chest area. This evolves into rhythmic head movements and bilateral arm flexion. In later phases, the patient displays left arm flexion and right arm extension, consistent with fencer posturing, while remaining unresponsive throughout the entire episode.

The patient is seated, holding a phone to their ear, and appears unresponsive to the surroundings. There is no observable seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or arm flexion/extension, and the patient remains still throughout the video. In other observations, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, remaining unresponsive throughout the episode. Additionally, the patient demonstrates episodes characterized by a blank stare, lip smacking, and slight head movements, with no visible progression to tonic-clonic activity, significant limb movements, or prominent automatisms.

The patient is lying still in bed, appearing unresponsive with eyes open and no visible signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. No observable movements or automatisms are present, and the patient remains still throughout the observation. At one point, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by head version to the left with left arm flexion. The seizure progresses to involve bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerking of the limbs. In another episode, the patient shows a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, which also progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of both arms. The patient remains unresponsive during all seizure episodes.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, initially appearing unresponsive with eyes closed and a blank stare. Seizure activity begins with lateralizing features: one episode starts with left arm flexion and right arm extension, another with right arm flexion and left arm extension, and a third with rightward head version and right arm flexion. These episodes progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events and shows no other signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking. During periods without seizure activity, the patient remains still and motionless.

The patient is seated in a chair or recliner, occasionally using a laptop, and at times receiving assistance from others or wearing supportive devices such as an oxygen mask or nasal cannula. Multiple episodes are characterized by a blank stare, often followed by automatisms including lip smacking and slight head movements. Some events involve asymmetric motor activity, including right arm flexion with left arm extension, unilateral arm flexion with possible tonic stiffening, or head version to the left accompanied by corresponding limb posturing. One episode includes a sudden forward bend with head down and arms reaching toward the floor. Tonic-clonic activity is observed in one instance, with tonic stiffening, arms flexed, hands clenched, and subsequent clonic jerks, during which the patient is unresponsive. In several observations, the patient remains unresponsive and shows no awareness of surroundings during the event, with no significant spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. There are also periods where the patient appears unresponsive but remains still, with no observable seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or limb movements. A head bandage is noted in one instance.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset in multiple observations, with subsequent motor manifestations varying across episodes. In one instance, the blank stare is followed by a slight head turn to the left, without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or other significant motor activity; the patient remains unresponsive and lies motionless under the blanket with no visible signs of movement or seizure evolution. In another episode, the blank stare is followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and continued unresponsiveness. A similar pattern includes right arm flexion and left arm extension evolving into bilateral arm flexion with clonic jerks, also without response. Additionally, head version to the left occurs with left arm flexion and right arm extension, forming a fencer posture, but without spread to generalized tonic-clonic activity. No observable early signs such as lip smacking are noted in any episode, and in one case, no seizure activity or movement is evident throughout the observation period.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm involvement, which may manifest as either flexion or extension. Left arm extension is also observed in some episodes. The seizure progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking of the limbs, including both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient is observed in a hospital bed, alternating between lying and sitting positions, appearing unresponsive and motionless throughout the recordings. At various times, the patient exhibits a blank stare, which in some instances is followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, suggesting possible focal impaired awareness seizure activity. Automatisms are noted, including raising the right hand to the face and covering the face with one or both hands, with subsequent movement of hands to the face and chest. In one segment, the patient displays signs of distress while covering their face, transitioning into a blank stare and appearing confused and unresponsive. The patient is also seen holding their hands together without any associated motor movements or observable seizure activity. Overall, no tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, lip smacking, or other generalized seizure manifestations are documented. Periods of calm and stillness without seizure activity are interspersed with episodes characterized by altered awareness and subtle motor features consistent with complex partial seizures.

The patient is lying in bed holding a tablet and at times appears responsive and engaged with the device, while at other times appears unresponsive and remains still. There are intervals with no observable seizure activity, including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or abnormal arm movements. During one episode, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, consistent with unilateral tonic posturing without significant bilateral spread. In another episode, the patient again shows a blank stare, followed by right arm extension and left arm flexion, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of both arms.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by one of the following focal motor manifestations: head version to the right with right arm extension, rhythmic head movements with arm flexion, or head version to the left with left arm flexion. In each instance, there is no visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic jerks. Additionally, there are periods during which the patient lies on a bed appearing unresponsive, either with a blank stare or eyes closed, showing no movement or signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks, and remains still throughout these intervals.

The patient is seated, either with their head slightly bowed and arms resting on their knees or in a hospital bed, appearing alert and engaged in conversation at times. Periodic episodes are observed featuring a blank stare followed by subtle motor manifestations including slight head movement, head tilt, rhythmic head movements, or rightward head version, often accompanied by subtle arm flexion—unilateral in some instances (right arm) and without spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. No visible automatisms such as lip smacking or significant unilateral/bilateral jerking are noted. At times, the patient transitions to a more relaxed posture with eyes closing and arms resting on the abdomen. Throughout the observation, the patient remains mostly calm and responsive, with no consistent signs of seizure activity in certain intervals. Seizure-like features are intermittent and localized, without progression to generalized motor activity.

The patient is observed in various positions—lying and seated—in a hospital bed, dressed in checkered pajamas with a bandage on their head. At one point, the patient is seen holding a phone to their ear with their right hand, appearing calm and engaged in a conversation. During the observation, the patient initially appears relaxed but subsequently exhibits signs of a seizure, including a blank stare, subtle flexion of the right hand, and progression to rhythmic jerking motions. In other segments, the patient is seated or lying still, showing no seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related movements, and appears to be in a state of rest.

The patient is seated in bed, occasionally holding a cup and eating or appearing engaged in conversation, but at other times appears unresponsive with a blank stare. During episodes of unresponsiveness, the patient exhibits rhythmic hand movements, including clapping, clenching, slight finger movements, and clasping hands together, accompanied by slight head movements or arm flexion in some instances. No significant spread to other body parts, bilateral tonic-clonic activity, automatisms (such as lip smacking), head version, or tonic stiffening is observed. The patient remains still at times with no visible movement or automatisms, while in other segments remains calm and responsive without seizure-like activity.

The patient lies in bed, covered with a blanket, and appears unresponsive or in a resting state, initially exhibiting a blank stare. There is no observable movement, change in posture, or signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, or arm flexion/extension. The patient remains still throughout the video.

The patient initially appears to be in a resting position and exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version—upward and to the left—and bilateral arm flexion with left arm predominance. This progresses to tonic stiffening and bilateral clonic movements, including rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, accompanied by backward tilting of the head and elevation of the arms in a clonic manner. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode, consistent with a bilateral tonic-clonic seizure.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lip smacking and head version to the right with right arm extension. The seizure progresses in some episodes to include right arm flexion and left arm extension, as well as bilateral arm involvement with rhythmic movements showing alternating flexion and extension, suggesting spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Clonic jerks and bilateral arm flexion are observed during progression, with the patient becoming unresponsive throughout the episodes. In certain instances, there is no visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic jerking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lateralized motor signs including left arm flexion with right arm extension or head version to the left with left arm flexion. The seizure may progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking and unresponsiveness. In some episodes, right arm flexion and left arm extension are observed, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare in multiple observations. There are no observable signs of lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient remains still throughout the video with no visible movement or change in position in most segments. One observation notes subtle lip movements and slight right hand motion with a brief clenching, suggesting possible early seizure activity. In another instance, the patient is seen initially holding a pen before placing both hands on the blanket, with no associated seizure activity noted. The patient is described as calm and unresponsive to surroundings, covered with a blanket, and wearing a blue shirt, in some reports specifying "RAMS" printed on it. No seizure activity is observed across the majority of the recordings.

The patient lies unresponsive in a hospital bed, with eyes open or closed at various times, exhibiting a blank stare in several episodes. During some events, the blank stare is followed by lateral head version—either to the left or right—accompanied by ipsilateral arm flexion. These focal motor features occur without visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, rhythmic jerks, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other automatisms such as lip smacking. In multiple segments, no observable seizure activity is present, including absence of blank stare, head version, arm flexion/extension, or any motor signs. The patient remains still throughout all videos, with no significant movement or progression to generalized seizure activity. Medical leads and a head bandage are noted in some instances. Overall, the episodes range from clinical seizures with focal onset and motor signs to periods of unresponsiveness without any detectable seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and asymmetric motor activity characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension, resembling fencer posturing. This is accompanied by rhythmic jerks in some episodes, but there is no progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observed period, lying in bed with head turned to the left, arms slightly flexed, and no visible response to external stimuli.

The patient initially appears to be resting, then exhibits a blank stare followed by slight head movement. The patient turns to the left side, partially lifting the head and arm, and remains in this position. In subsequent events, the patient consistently exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, with progression to left arm extension and right arm extension resembling fencer posturing in one instance. Rhythmic jerks ensue during one of these episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with minimal movement observed in some cases.

The patient is lying on a hospital bed, initially appearing unresponsive with their head slightly tilted back and no visible movement or signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, or limb flexion/extension. In some episodes, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by variable motor manifestations: one episode shows a slight head turn to the left without significant movement or spread; another shows leftward head version with left arm flexion progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening and clonic jerks involving both arms; two separate episodes involve right arm flexion and left arm extension. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events. In contrast, one observation notes the patient initially lying still, then becoming alert and responsive, shifting position, raising an arm, and sitting up in bed.

The patient is lying in bed, holding a cup, and appears to be interacting with it. Initially, there is no observable seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, or bilateral tonic-clonic movements, and the patient remains relatively still and unresponsive to external stimuli. Subsequently, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and slight extension, without significant spread to other body regions.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare. Subtle movements of the right hand are observed initially, progressing to more pronounced right arm flexion and slight head deviation to the right in some segments. Slight head movement and subtle arm flexion occur without significant spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or rhythmic clonic jerks. In other segments, there are no visible signs of head version, arm flexion, or clonic jerks, and the patient remains still throughout the observation period.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by slight head movement and subtle hand gestures or arm flexion. Initial movements may include right arm extension with slight head version to the right, progressing to bilateral arm involvement characterized by rhythmic jerking and clonic movements. The motor activity evolves from focal onset in the right arm to both arms, with asynchronous clonic jerks—featuring flexion of the left arm and extension of the right—and increasing amplitude and frequency of flexion-extension motions. No significant spread to generalized bilateral tonic-clonic activity or pronounced automatisms is observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic head movements and arm flexion. In some episodes, the movements progress to involve both arms with alternating flexion and extension, indicating a spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events. Rhythmic clonic jerks in both arms are noted, sometimes asynchronous, with the right arm showing more pronounced movement. One episode features initial right arm flexion and left arm extension before evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity. In other instances, there is no clear progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient is seated in a hospital chair, wearing a hospital gown and a head bandage, initially appearing relaxed and occasionally looking around. The patient engages in eating from a tray or plate, using utensils and remaining responsive and attentive to the activity. At times, the patient is observed using a laptop on a nearby table. There are periods of rest with no observable movement or interaction. The patient attempts to stand while eating, appearing unsteady, leaning forward and to the side, but returns to a seated position without loss of coordination or observable seizure activity during these movements. Throughout most of the observation, there is no visible seizure activity, including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or limb jerking. The patient remains alert, responsive, and engaged in eating or resting, with no signs of automatisms or involuntary movements. However, during one distinct episode, the patient suddenly stops eating, looks down, and appears to lose consciousness. This is followed by a blank stare and onset of involuntary motor activity, beginning with right arm flexion and left arm extension. The movement progresses to unilateral then bilateral involvement, with tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms. The seizure evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic activity, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout the entire episode. No other seizure-related behaviors such as lip smacking or head version are noted outside this event.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare. There is no visible movement or signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, arm flexion, extension, or other seizure activity. The patient's arms are resting on the bed, and there is no significant motor activity or progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains still throughout the observation.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable motor manifestations including subtle arm flexion, bilateral arm flexion with slight extension, and right arm flexion with slight head version to the right. The movements remain localized without spreading to other body parts and do not progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, with bilateral spread of movements. The arm movements include rhythmic flexion and extension. A slight head turn to the right is noted initially. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode, with no significant additional motor activity or progression to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures observed.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare and no visible movement initially. One episode shows progression from the blank stare to right arm flexion and left arm extension, followed by bilateral arm flexion with rhythmic jerking, indicating spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. In another instance, the blank stare is followed by rhythmic jerking of both arms without clear lateralization or progression to tonic-clonic movements. No signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, or spontaneous arm movements are observed during periods of stillness, and the patient remains motionless throughout the video when not exhibiting seizure activity.

The patient is observed sitting or seated upright in a hospital bed, variously holding a phone, device, toy, remote control, or bag, and appears engaged with the object or in conversation. When using the phone, the patient holds it to their ear with the right hand while the left hand rests on the lap, grasps the cord, or is raised near the face; occasional adjustments of the phone and touching the face are noted. In other instances, the patient is focused on a device or toy, either independently or while interacting with another person, showing sustained attention and responsiveness without agitation or discomfort. Throughout these observations, there are no observable signs of seizure activity, including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains relatively still and behaviorally stable during these periods. In contrast, distinct episodes of seizure activity are documented. One episode features a blank stare followed by rhythmic head movements and asymmetric limb involvement—right arm flexion and left arm extension—without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Another episode progresses from a blank stare to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. Additionally, one observation describes the patient lying unresponsive on the bed with left arm flexed (held by another person), head turned to the left, and right arm bent at the elbow near the face; this state shows no evolution into generalized rhythmic jerking or bilateral convulsive activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lip smacking and head version that varies between rightward and leftward. This is accompanied by asymmetric motor activity including right arm extension, unilateral right arm flexion, left arm flexion, and progression to bilateral arm involvement with tonic stiffening. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episodes, during which others attempt to provide support.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head version to the right. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observed period.

The patient is lying in bed, initially appearing relaxed. Suddenly, the patient's head tilts backward and the eyes close, indicating a possible loss of consciousness; however, no visible limb movements, tonic-clonic activity, automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks are observed during this brief episode. Following this, the patient remains unresponsive with eyes closed and exhibits no observable movement or signs of seizure activity throughout the remainder of the observation period. There are no indications of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, or other motor manifestations typically associated with seizures. The patient remains still and unresponsive for the duration of the video.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lip smacking and slight head movement in some episodes, with no visible spread to tonic-clonic movements or significant automatisms. In other instances, the patient exhibits a blank stare accompanied by head version to the left and left arm flexion, remaining unresponsive throughout. During multiple observed periods, the patient lies on a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare, showing no visible movement, change in posture, or signs of seizure activity—including no automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, rhythmic jerking, limb jerking, head version, or arm flexion/extension. The patient remains still or in a resting position throughout these episodes, with no observable seizure activity.

The patient is lying on a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with eyes closed and head tilted back. The posture is relaxed, with arms at the sides, and there is no visible movement or response to external stimuli throughout the video. The patient remains in a consistent, still position without any observable signs of seizure activity, including absence seizures, blank stare, lip smacking, head version, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or limb flexion/extension. One segment notes a distinct episode involving a blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension resembling fencer posturing, during which the patient remained unresponsive.
